module_map.responsibility: Central request dispatching: loads middleware chain, resolves requests, dispatches to views, supports both sync (WSGI) and async (ASGI) execution modes via adapt_method_mode
HTTP primitives: HttpRequest (including multipart parsing, META dict, session/cookie access), HttpResponse hierarchy (streaming, file, JSON), cookie handling
URL routing: pattern compilation (RoutePattern, RegexPattern), resolver tree traversal, reverse URL lookup, URL converters, namespace resolution
ORM and database layer: model queries via QuerySet/Manager, migrations, database backends, transactions, connection pooling
Request/response pipeline hooks: security headers, CSRF protection, session, cache, locale, gzip, common redirects. All extend MiddlewareMixin from utils.deprecation
View callables and class-based views: generic views (ListView, DetailView, FormView etc.), decorators, error handlers, debug views
Settings management via lazy-loaded LazySettings wrapper; global_settings provides defaults. Central configuration point for the entire framework
Cross-cutting utilities: encoding, functional helpers (lazy evaluation, cached_property), deprecation/MiddlewareMixin, logging helpers, datastructures, async wrappers
Blinker-style signal/receiver system: Signal.send(), Signal.send_robust(), receiver decorator for decoupled event hooks
Application registry: discovers and loads installed apps, resolves models by label, provides app-level ready() lifecycle hook
Template engines: compilation, rendering context, template loaders, custom tags/filters via Library, response classes
Form validation pipeline: Field validation, Widget rendering, Form/ModelForm binding, formsets, error handling

pipeline_trace.description: WSGI environ or ASGI scope/receive is converted into an HttpRequest object by WSGIHandler.__call__ or ASGIHandler. The handler then calls get_response / get_response_async, entering the middleware chain.

BaseHandler.load_middleware builds an onion of callables. Each middleware wraps the next via __call__ or process_request. Sync middlewares are auto-wrapped with sync_to_async when running in async mode via adapt_method_mode.
BaseHandler.resolve_request calls get_resolver(request.urlconf).resolve(path). URLResolver walks the pattern tree, returning a ResolverMatch containing the view callable, args, and kwargs.
The resolved callback is called with (request, *args, **kwargs). Class-based views dispatch to get/post/etc. methods. make_view_atomic wraps the view in a DB transaction if ATOMIC_REQUESTS is set.
Views interact with Model.objects (Manager) to build QuerySets. QuerySet is lazy; SQL is compiled and executed when iterated or evaluated. Results are mapped back to model instances.
Views return an HttpResponse (or TemplateResponse which defers rendering). TemplateResponse.render() merges context with a Template to produce the final content bytes.
Middlewares with process_response are called in reverse order (outermost last). Each can mutate or replace the response. check_response validates that a proper HttpResponse was returned.
Final HttpResponse is serialized: status line, headers, and body chunks are written back to the WSGI start_response + iterable or ASGI send coroutine. log_response records the final status.

cross_module_hubs.reason: Nearly every module imports `from django.conf import settings` to read configuration. It has no upstream Django dependencies itself, making it a pure root dependency. Its LazySettings pattern means it is safe to import at module level across the entire codebase.

Provides foundational cross-cutting utilities consumed by almost all other packages: MiddlewareMixin (middleware), functional.py (lazy evaluation, cached_property), encoding.py, datastructures.MultiValueDict (http), log.py (handlers), deprecation.py, and module_loading.import_string. It is the widest fan-in module after conf.

HttpRequest and HttpResponse are the primary data types passed between every layer: handlers, middleware, views, template responses, test client, and the URL resolver. Any module that touches the request/response lifecycle imports from django.http, making it the central type hub of the pipeline.

change_proposal.feature_summary: Add an async-compatible structured logging middleware (StructuredLoggingMiddleware) that measures per-request wall time, captures the resolved view name from ResolverMatch, counts ORM queries via django.db.connection.queries, and emits a single structured JSON log entry on response.
files_to_modify: django/utils/log.py (optionally extend log_response or add a structured_log_request helper)
new_types: StructuredLoggingMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin): async-aware middleware class with __call__ / __acall__ that wraps the inner handler, records start time, delegates, then emits the log entry
RequestLogRecord(TypedDict): typed dict capturing view_name (str), method (str), path (str), status_code (int), duration_ms (float), query_count (int), user (str | None)
change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: Follow MiddlewareMixin from django/utils/deprecation.py: define both sync __call__ and async __acall__ (or use adapt_method_mode pattern from BaseHandler). Inspect the existing SecurityMiddleware and CacheMiddleware in django/middleware/ for the process_request / process_response hooks pattern. Use django.utils.log.log_response as a reference for how to emit per-request log records with request and response context.
change_proposal.integration_point: Add 'django.middleware.structured_logging.StructuredLoggingMiddleware' near the top of MIDDLEWARE in settings (after SecurityMiddleware, before CommonMiddleware) so it wraps the full view lifecycle and can measure total time including inner middleware overhead. It reads request.resolver_match.view_name after the inner chain resolves (available post-dispatch) and reads len(django.db.connection.queries) when DEBUG=True or via a custom query counting context manager.
risks: ORM query count via connection.queries requires DEBUG=True; in production a custom db instrumentation signal or django.test.utils.CaptureQueriesContext pattern would be needed, adding complexity.
Async compatibility: MiddlewareMixin's async path requires careful use of sync_to_async/async_to_sync; if the middleware accidentally runs blocking code in async context it will deadlock under ASGI.
resolver_match is only populated after URL dispatch; if a 404 occurs before resolve the view_name will be None and must be handled gracefully.
Structured log volume: emitting one log record per request at INFO level can produce high log throughput under load; downstream log aggregation infrastructure must be sized appropriately.
Django's existing logging configuration (LOGGING in settings) uses dictConfig; adding a new logger name requires documentation and may conflict with user-defined logging trees.

tool_usage.params: path=/tmp/benchmark-repos/django/django, max_depth=2, summary=true — top-level module structure overview
path=django/core/handlers/base.py — BaseHandler middleware loading and request dispatch logic
path=django/utils/log.py — existing log_response helper and logging infrastructure
